synthesis_text: Start by correlating the 502/504 spikes with backend latency: collect gateway error logs with timestamps (N1:0) and enable end‑to‑end tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to measure each downstream HTTP call and DB query latency (N1:1). Compare post‑release metrics (request rate, DB CPU, thread‑pool usage) against a known good baseline (N1:2) to spot abnormal resource consumption. Profile the database: turn on the slow‑query log (N2:0), run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on high‑CPU queries (N2:1) and check for lock contention (N2:2) to identify missing indexes or inefficient scans (N1:4). Verify backend thread/worker pool sizes and queue lengths (N1:5) and ensure the application connection‑pool is not saturated (N2:3). Identify any downstream services that are slow or failing and add appropriate timeouts and circuit‑breaker settings (N1:3) to prevent hangs. Adjust gateway and load‑balancer timeout settings if backend processing exceeds defaults (N3:5). If the issue persists, temporarily roll back the release or disable new features (N1:6) to confirm the code change impact, and consider rate‑limiting or back‑pressure at the gateway (N1:7) to protect downstream services during traffic spikes.

Begin by correlating the 502/504 timestamps with gateway logs (N1:0:194aea7d, N3:0:0c81ec28). Enable end‑to‑end tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to measure latency of each downstream HTTP call and database query (N1:1:2b6d6315, N3:1:4be134e8). Compare post‑release metrics with baseline to identify spikes in request rate, DB CPU, and thread/worker pool usage (N1:2:36c3bcfd). Profile the database: enable the slow‑query log (N2:0:137d8edf), run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on high‑CPU statements to spot full scans or missing indexes (N2:1:5cf867db), inspect lock contention (N2:2:bc8ef45e), and add covering indexes or rewrite queries to reduce CPU load (N2:4:43cdf47f). Verify application and DB connection‑pool sizes and increase them if they are saturated (N1:5:20ef186b). Add timeout and circuit‑breaker settings for downstream services to prevent hangs (N1:3:2c89eaa7, N3:7:45d33d16) and implement rate limiting or back‑pressure at the gateway (N1:7:942dc067). If the problem persists, temporarily roll back the release or disable new features to confirm code impact (N1:6:19bfa72f), and consider scaling the database vertically or adding read replicas (N3:8:d0898ba6) plus caching frequent read queries (N3:9:89342830) to lower load and stabilize response times.

To locate the bottleneck, first collect gateway error logs with timestamps (N1:0:194aea7d, N3:0:0c81ec28) and correlate them with backend latency spikes (N3:2:1b2e042b). Enable distributed tracing (N1:1:2b6d6315, N3:1:4be134e8) to measure latency of each downstream HTTP call and database query. Compare post‑release metrics with baseline (N1:2:36c3bcfd) to see changes in request rate, DB CPU, connection‑pool usage, and thread counts. Profile the database: enable the slow‑query log (N2:0:137d8edf), run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on high‑CPU queries (N2:1:5cf867db), inspect lock contention (N2:2:bc8ef45e), and verify the application connection‑pool size (N2:3:00debf2e). Identify any slow or failing downstream services and add appropriate timeouts and circuit‑breaker settings (N1:3:b91f3a96, N3:7:45d33d16) to prevent hangs. Verify backend thread/worker pool sizes and queue lengths (N1:5:20ef186b) and increase them if saturation is observed. Check gateway and load‑balancer timeout settings (N3:5:fc80b594) and raise them if backend processing exceeds defaults. If errors disappear after a temporary rollback or disabling new features (N1:6:19bfa72f), the release is likely the cause. To stabilize the system, implement rate limiting or back‑pressure at the gateway (N1:7:942dc067), scale the database vertically or add read replicas (N3:8:d0898ba6), and add caching for frequent read queries (N3:9:89342830).
